data.title: Families appeal for information on loved ones as scores missing in Hong Kong fire
data.description:
Scores of people, including infants and the elderly, remain unaccounted for a day after Hong Kong’s deadliest blaze in decades ravaged Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po. Families and friends are racing against time, issuing appeals at the scene and on social media to find their loved ones.
The Post put together some of the appeals as the search and rescue operation continues, after authorities reported that 279 people were missing.
Six-month-old Ho Tsz-yan and her grandparents
Winnie Hui tells the Post that her last phone chat with her 68-year-old mother-in-law, Li Kin-yuk, was at 3.02pm on Wednesday, while Li was caring for her six-month-old baby girl Ho Tsz-yan, at her flat in Wang Cheong House.
“[Li] said that there was smoke outside and would move from my flat numbered 2104 to another numbered 2101. But I lost contact with them ever since,” she said, adding that her father-in-law, 75, was also missing.
In her online appeal, she said that her daughter has a dark blue birthmark on her foot and expressed worry that the girl could not survive without milk for more than 24 hours.
Hannah Cheng, 5, and a domestic helper

At the fire scene, a desperate mother who lives in Wang Tai House was distributing photos of her 5-year-old daughter Hannah Cheng and domestic helper Maryan who went missing after the fire.
The leaflet obtained by the Post said the girl was in a white top and grey long pants, while her domestic worker is 155cm (5 feet 1 inch) tall and larger-bodied.
A 16-year-old female student and two relatives

Her boyfriend, with an Instagram name yinjjisbigbiggoodgood107, appealed online that the 16-year-old, her grandmother in her 70s and her uncle lost contact on Wednesday afternoon. The three lived together in flat 1605 of one of the eight blocks.
He showed screen captures of his WhatsApp conversations with his girlfriend, who returned home after a school examination. She texted him: “Fire in the next unit is coming … I am suffering.” Her last message was at 3.23pm on Wednesday.
A 15-month-old girl, two relatives and a domestic helper
An Instagram user named be_ruby made repeated appeals on Threads on Thursday afternoon that she lost contact with a 15-month-old baby girl, her two relatives, and a domestic helper, all living in flat 1907 of Wang Tai House.
So Yan-yip, 84, and wife Tsang Yuk-may, 83

Another Instagram user named Jimmyltm said his relative’s parents-in-law, who live in flat 2701 of Wang Cheong House, went missing at 3pm on Wednesday.
Additional reporting by Fiona Sun

data.title: ‘Tired and anxious’: displaced Hong Kong fire survivors facing sleepless nights
data.description:
Residents displaced by Hong Kong’s worst fire in seven decades have been left weary and anxious, fearing another sleepless night and uncertain about where they can shelter.
A 76-year-old retiree, Chow, who lives in the Kwong Fuk public housing estate in Tai Po, spent all of Thursday sitting in the open area near Wang Fuk Court – the scene of the fire – after being evacuated from her home the previous day.
“My back hurts from sitting too long on the hard flower bed edge. I did not sleep at all last night in the restaurant,” said Chow.
“I feel very tired and I might get sick from it. I think two nights are the maximum that I can go through.”
About 500 residents were staying at nine temporary shelters in Tai Po, the government said on Thursday.
While Chow felt lucky her home was unharmed, she said lingering smoke forced people to stay outdoors overnight.
She and her neighbours had their fingers crossed that they would be allowed to return home on Thursday night. Food and clothing were sufficient, but the biggest challenge was the lack of a comfortable place to sleep.
For those living in Wang Fuk Court, the chances of returning home were slim, as the fire had not yet been extinguished 24 hours after it started at 2.51pm on Wednesday.
As of 3pm on Thursday, three out of seven residential blocks that caught fire were still burning, with only one of the eight blocks left unscathed.
Many residents left the shelters for their relatives’ homes, uncertain how long their temporary arrangements would last before they needed to find long‑term solutions.
Ho, a retiree in her sixties, left a nearby shelter with her husband and grandchildren around 2.30pm after registering her details to receive updates on future arrangements.

“We were on our way to the restaurant yesterday when we noticed the nearby building was on fire,” Ho said. “We brought our mother down in her wheelchair as quickly as we could because we knew the smoke might affect the operation of the lift.”
The family spent the night at a relative’s home and planned to stay there for now, though they remained unsure about what would come next.
Ho said the hope of going back soon was “getting smaller and smaller”, as they could see from a distance how bad the damage was, adding that they had not yet thought about a long-term plan.
Another resident of Wang Fuk Court, a retiree surnamed Tsang, also planned to stay with a friend while seeking longer-term accommodation. He was walking his dog near Wang Shing House at around 3pm on Wednesday when he noticed flames breaking out in another building.
“I saw a lot of smoke and flames. When I tried to go back upstairs, the firefighters had already blocked the way and were telling everyone to leave,” said Tsang, who is in his seventies.
He added he felt “lucky enough” that his wife and daughter were not at home at the time, and that he had taken his dog with him.
In a statement to the media, the Hong Kong Housing Society said it was fully cooperating with the Housing Bureau to support those affected by the fire.
It will arrange flats from its transitional housing scheme to meet the urgent needs of residents, including about 90 homes from its Trackside Villas project in Tai Po.
The society added that all Wang Fuk Court residents requiring help could call its 24-hour support line at 2839 1393 to reach staff members.
The death toll reached 55 as of 2.50pm on Thursday, making it one of the deadliest fires in Hong Kong’s history. At that time a total of 76 people were in hospital, with 15 in critical condition and 28 in serious condition.
The number of missing persons remained unconfirmed, and rescue work continued as flames retreated to the upper floors of three blocks.

data.title: Hong Kong insurers liable for payouts of US$334 million related to deadly Tai Po fire
data.description:
Hong Kong’s insurance sector is expected to face record claims of HK$2.6 billion (US$334 million) related to the deadly fire in Tai Po, the worst in seven decades that claimed 55 lives and seriously damaged seven buildings, according to industry insiders.
The fire would lead to a surge in insurance payments for personal and property coverage in the coming year, they said.
The Insurance Authority on Thursday set up a special task force led by senior executives to ensure all insurance companies sped up their compensation processes for claims related to the fire in which nearly 280 people were still missing. Among those hospitalised, 45 were in serious condition.
“Many insurance companies have already taken immediate action, such as setting up hotlines, as well as simplifying and speeding up the claims process to provide additional help to those affected to cope with the tough times,” said Stephen Yiu Kin-wah, chairman of the Insurance Authority, in a statement.


Thousands of victims lost their homes after a massive blaze broke out on Wednesday in seven of eight buildings under renovation at Wang Fuk Court in the northern New Territories. The blaze was only brought under control on Thursday afternoon.
Members of the Hong Kong Federation of Insurers were taking proactive measures to help, such as waiving the need for death certificates to process death claims and offering holidays and grace periods on premium payment, according to a statement by the industry guild.
“Our member companies are fully equipped to assist victims in their time of need, providing resources and support to help them through this difficult journey towards recovery,” said Selina Lau, the federation’s CEO.
The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) and the Hong Kong Association of Banks were also taking steps to help the victims. They urged banks to adopt a flexible approach to assist those who need funds, including prioritising loan applications and waiving late repayment fees.
The HKMA urged banks to add staff at their branches in Tai Po to provide prompt service.
State-owned China Taiping Insurance (HK) was the insurer for Wang Fuk Court, according to the minutes of a meeting from November 15, 2024, posted on the estate’s website. The estate renewed the coverage with China Taiping – paying a premium of HK$337,737 for two years until the end of 2026 – which gave it a coverage of HK$2 billion for property and fire-related damages.
The policy includes public liability of HK$20 million per incident of death or property loss and other related coverage.
China Taiping also provided insurance cover of HK$315.6 million for “contractors’ risks” and HK$50 million of third-party liability for Prestige Construction & Engineering, which was responsible for the renovation of the estate.
The insurer’s shares slumped as much as 8 per cent on Thursday before closing 2 per cent lower at HK$17.90.
China Taiping did not immediately respond to the Post’s queries.

Other insurers might also face claims from individual victims who had purchased life, medical and home content insurance, with the total payout likely to reach HK$2.6 billion, according to several industry insiders who did not want to be identified.
Yiu said policyholders who needed help could call the hotline set up by the Insurance Authority at 38999983, or the Hong Kong Federation of Insurers at 28619367.
“From the scale of the fire, it is likely insurers will see record claims,” said Tom Chan Pak-lam, the permanent honorary president of the Institute of Securities Dealers, an industry body for brokers.
“The price of fire insurance-related policies will inevitably rise next year after insurers pay such high compensation,” he added.
The last major property insurance claim in the city was related to a fire at the under-construction Kimpton Hotel in February 2023. The claim was estimated to be in the region of HK$200 million to HK$500 million.

data.title: Baidu cuts jobs across the board, restructures AI teams after loss-making quarter
data.description:
Chinese tech giant Baidu is cutting an undisclosed number of jobs across major divisions and restructuring artificial intelligence teams, as the company moves to streamline operations following a loss-making quarter, according to multiple sources.
Baidu has axed staff in Beijing, Shanghai and Guangzhou, affecting multiple major business units from its mobile ecosystem group, which includes its bread-and-butter search business, to intelligent cloud and technology platform groups, according to employees affected by the move.
One Beijing-based employee in the mobile ecosystems unit said four of the nine people on his team received severance notices in separate meetings with human resource managers this week, and were expected to leave in December.
The affected employees were given compensation packages that included one month’s pay for each year of service, and between one and three months of additional salary, according to one employee, who added that it was the largest lay-off at Baidu in recent years.
Another employee in Guangzhou said he signed the severance agreement, despite an initial hesitance, because the compensation package was reasonable.
It was not immediately known how many employees would be laid off. Baidu did not respond to multiple requests for comment on Thursday.
The Beijing-based company has been downsizing its payroll in recent years. It had 35,900 employees at the end of 2024, a drop of 9.8 per cent from the previous year.

Baidu’s job cuts come after the company posted a third-quarter loss in spite of a 50 per cent jump in AI-related revenue in the three months ended September 30.
However, the AI surge did not offset a lacklustre quarter as revenue for the period declined 7 per cent year on year, owing to sluggish demand in its advertising business amid slower overall economic growth.
For the quarter, the company reported an 11.2 billion yuan (US$1.6 billion) loss on asset write-downs during the period.
Baidu also restructured its AI model development by establishing two separate units for foundational models and application models, according to a Wednesday report by local media outlet Jiemian.
The new units, reporting directly to founder and CEO Robin Li Yanhong, will be responsible for the company’s Ernie AI model series. Responsibility for the company’s flagship AI model has been handed to a younger cohort of managers, mirroring a strategy seen at DeepSeek and ByteDance, where rising talent is being pushed to the forefront of AI research.
Baidu also replaced Xue Su as head of its ChatGPT-like Ernie bot app, known as Wenxin in China, according to one source familiar with the matter.
The company, which was among the first in China to launch a challenger to OpenAI’s ChatGPT, was also losing chatbot users.
Ernie was ranked the ninth most popular AI chatbot by monthly active users in September, trailing the top three of ByteDance’s Doubao, DeepSeek’s namesake chat app and Tencent Holding’s Yuanbao, according to data published last month by analytics firm QuestMobile.
Baidu was also facing stiff competition from other AI models in the Chinese market, said Wang Kai, senior equity analyst at investment consultancy Morningstar.
“The company is trying to be as flexible as possible given that large language models are increasingly becoming more commoditised,” Wang said.

data.title: Elderly China woman seeks ‘daughter’ to care for her, offers a flat, US$420 monthly salary
data.description:
An elderly woman from central China has turned to the internet in search of a new “daughter” to care for her in place of her biological daughters, offering a flat and a monthly salary in return.
The woman, from Henan province and known by her surname Ma, shared the motivations behind her search during a local TV programme on November 19.
Ma explained that she has two biological daughters, one of whom wishes to sever ties with her, while the other suffers from a mental disability and is unable to care for herself.

Struggling with asthma and difficulty walking even 100 metres, Ma hopes to find a woman who can provide care, accompany her to medical appointments, and treat her with the warmth of a daughter.
She is willing to offer the “daughter” one of her two flats, along with her possessions and her monthly pension of 3,000 yuan (US$420) as a monthly salary.
According to the Chinese media outlet Dazhong News, Ma has additional savings of 400,000 yuan (US$56,000).
She mentioned her plan to leave her other flat to her younger daughter.
Reportedly, Ma’s elder daughter has distanced herself due to disagreements over the upbringing of Ma’s granddaughter.
The elder daughter has claimed she is unemployed and unable to look after her mother, stating that Ma’s decisions are “none of her business.”
Ma also divorced their father at a young age and has lost contact with most of her relatives.
In her search, she expressed a willingness to sign a contract with her future “daughter.”

Reactions from the online community have been mixed. Some users expressed interest in applying, while others raised concerns about the arrangement’s viability.
“It seems to me that she is seeking someone to care for both herself and her younger daughter,” one commenter observed.
“Given that she won’t even bequeath her property to her elder daughter, I doubt she would treat a new ‘daughter’ well,” remarked another.
“With declining housing prices, the flat and cash she offers may not be enough to afford even a nanny for two people,” a third commenter pointed out.
Lawyer Xi Junqi from the Henan Zhongdi Law Firm stated that Ma’s elder daughter has a legal obligation to support her mother, which she cannot evade by renouncing her inheritance rights. He added that anyone who agrees to be Ma’s “daughter” would need to enter into a legacy support agreement.

A nationwide survey conducted by the Ministry of Civil Affairs in 2021 revealed that nearly 60 per cent of individuals aged 60 and over in mainland China are empty nesters.
This situation has fuelled a burgeoning industry of hiring “children” – individuals paid to temporarily act as companions for the elderly, accompanying them to medical appointments and assisting with daily challenges, such as resolving disputes with caretakers.
These outsourced companions reportedly charge between 500 and 2,500 yuan (US$70 to US$350) per visit, depending on the duration of service.

data.title: The Nobel Prize for Physics that proves big things can be quantum too
data.description:
In the eighth instalment of his exclusive series for the South China Morning Post, American theoretical physicist and Nobel laureate Frank Wilczek explores the big world manifestation of small world quantum mechanics. Read his previous articles here.
The Nobel Prize in Physics for 2025 was awarded to John Clarke, Michel Devoret and John Martinis “for the discovery of macroscopic quantum tunnelling and energy quantisation in an electric circuit”. The trio collaborated on a series of groundbreaking experiments during 1984 and 1985.
The phrase “quantum leap” is often used, in common language and popular culture, to mean a big change. In nature, though, individual quantum leaps are generally both tiny and unpredictable. This year’s Nobel Prize was awarded for the creation of controlled, tangible quantum leaps in electric circuits.
Their work laid the foundation for a major approach for building a new kind of computer: the quantum computer. Eventually, quantum computers could take much of the guesswork out of chemistry and materials design.
While the promise of quantum computing has not yet been fully realised, the superconducting-circuit approach inspired by Clarke, Devoret and Martinis has made spectacular progress in recent years and leads the field so far.
To appreciate the Nobel committee’s citation, we need to grasp the two central ideas of quantum theory that it highlights: quantum tunnelling and energy quantisation.
What is quantum tunnelling? I’ll answer that question by way of a very important example.
Our sun derives its energy by “burning” nuclear fuel. The energy-releasing reactions involve fusion, the merger of two separate nuclei into one larger one. In the most basic of these fusion reactions, two protons combine into a single nucleus with smaller mass.

In detail, the two protons combine into a deuteron, while emitting a neutrino and a positron. Since a deuteron weighs less than two separate protons, the merger releases energy, according to Einstein’s E = mc² formula.
Fusion requires the protons to come extremely close together, but they repel one another electrically. Even in the sun’s core, where the temperature is about 15 million degrees, protons don’t move fast enough to plough through this electrical barrier.
Here – luckily for us – quantum tunnelling comes to the rescue. According to quantum theory, it is not always adequate to think of protons as small particles with definite positions that follow mechanical trajectories. Rather, a proton’s position is described by a “wavicle” of probability that extends through space.
The proton’s wavicle can leak – or, as we usually say, tunnel – through regions where a classical particle wouldn’t have enough energy to penetrate. Thus quantum physics allows particles to surmount otherwise impassable barriers.
It is quantum tunnelling that allows wavicle protons in the sun to overcome their mutual repulsion, consummate their union – and give birth to sunshine. Without this consequence of quantum tunnelling, Earth would be cold and lifeless.
Quantum tunnelling is central to many other phenomena in physics, chemistry and biology. It has already been honoured in several Nobel Prizes – from the 1973 awards to Leo Esaki, Ivar Giaever and Brian Josephson for tunnelling in solids, through the 1986 prize to Gerd Binnig and Heinrich Rohrer for the scanning tunnelling microscope, to chemistry prizes in 1992 and 2013 recognising electron tunnelling reactions in molecules and enzymes.
Why has quantum tunnelling been central to so many Nobel Prizes? Basically, because it transforms the impossible into the merely improbable. The weakness and fragility of quantum tunnelling is, ironically, also the source of its power and versatility: tiny changes in conditions can change tunnelling rates enormously.
Scientists exploit this sensitivity to create versatile sensors and regulators that respond to minute shifts in their surroundings. Evolution has mastered it too. Enzymes in living cells steer chemical reactions, and through them metabolism and physiology, by modulating tunnelling rates.
I expect that future Nobel Prizes will arise from better understanding how quantum tunnelling proceeds in space and time, from elucidating its suspected role in directing photosynthesis and from the invention of new devices that exploit its power and versatility. These advances will make human engineering more versatile and efficient by making it, literally, more lifelike.


Energy quantisation, discovered through the work of Planck, Einstein and Bohr, lies at the root of quantum theory. In a hydrogen atom, a proton and an electron are bound together by electrical attraction. If we imagined them as classical particles, the possible energies would form a continuum.
But in reality only a discrete set of levels is observed. Using quantum wavicles, the explanation is beautifully musical: just as a bell rings in distinct tones, the electron-proton system vibrates in discrete patterns, each with its characteristic energy. The atom can only exchange energy with the outside world in definite units – quanta – by passing from one vibration pattern into another.
Other atoms, molecules, materials and even “elementary” particles made from quarks and gluons support different kinds of vibration patterns among different species of wavicles. But all fall within the same musical framework, as different instruments in nature’s orchestra.
Before wavicles were understood, Niels Bohr explained quantisation by postulating “allowed” orbits. In that early language, energy changes took the form of abrupt quantum leaps between orbits.
In modern theory, everything evolves continuously in time, though sometimes very fast. There are no true leaps – only waves that shift and interfere. But the vivid picture of a quantum leap is too appealing to abandon, and it is not so wrong that we need to.
With those key concepts of quantum tunnelling and energy quantisation in mind, we can appreciate the achievement of John Clarke, Michel Devoret and John Martinis concretely. In all the natural contexts we mentioned above, the “quantum leaps” involved very small natural systems and tiny amounts of energy.
They showed that quantum tunnelling and energy quantisation also occur for electric currents in processes involving the collective motion of billions of electrons. They built superconducting circuits that display these effects – macroscopic systems behaving as quantum wavicles.
It wasn’t easy. They had to work at temperatures within a few thousandths of a degree of absolute zero, to isolate their circuits from outside influences and to gently amplify small signals. In overcoming those challenges, they introduced many innovations. They paved a path that others have been able to follow and to widen.


Quantum theory teaches us that the world, when viewed at high resolution in space and time, is a wonderland of wavicles in ceaseless activity. Most of this detail is averaged over, and hidden, in objects large enough for unaided humans to handle or even to perceive. Superconducting circuits bring it back into view. They let us see the world as it truly is – and perhaps to build computers that can do the same.
Transformed from mystery into machinery, even the weirdest features of quantum theory are becoming tools we can use. To paraphrase astronaut Neil Armstrong, that’s a small step for a current, but a giant leap for mankind.
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: As Asia’s oil markets eye Ukraine-Russia peace deal, will prices fall further?
data.description:
Asia’s oil-dependent economies could soon find further relief as a peace deal between Moscow and Kyiv edges closer, raising the prospects of Russia resuming crude supply to global markets and again reshaping supply dynamics that have significantly influenced prices since the war in Ukraine broke out in 2022.
Analysts told This Week in Asia that the potential accord had already led to lower oil prices amid heightened expectations that sanctions against Moscow might soon be lifted, potentially unleashing large volumes of crude from Russia, the world’s second-largest producer, in international markets.
Oil prices slipped again this week as traders reacted to news of the US-brokered proposed deal, even though negotiating teams have yet to reach an agreement on its most sensitive provisions.
Brent crude oil prices fell by 0.4 per cent to US$62.86 a barrel in the afternoon in Asia on Thursday, while US West Texas Intermediate crude dropped by a similar margin to US$58.42 a barrel.
Crude oil prices are on track to fall in November for a fourth consecutive month, the longest losing streak since 2023.
The prospect of more supplies than demand in the event of a peace deal to end the war in Ukraine has been weighing on the oil market.
Prices initially declined by as much as two per cent in response to news of an imminent peace deal, and swung both ways thereafter as markets priced in lingering uncertainty.

US envoy Steve Witkoff is set to travel to Moscow next week with other senior US officials for talks with Russian leaders on Washington’s plan to end the war.
“Crucially, the devil is still in the details. What are being described as ‘minor points to be resolved’ will determine whether this proposal is accepted by both sides, especially as hostilities have continued in the form of deadly strikes,” said Jorge Leon, head of Rystad Energy’s geopolitical analysis.
The key issues include finalising the territorial borders between Russia and Ukraine, security guarantees and implementation timelines, he said.
“For energy markets, this means volatility is far from over. Prices reacted swiftly to the initial optimism for an agreement, but the underlying uncertainty has not changed,” Leon said.
As for the longer term, oil prices remain bearish, according to analysts.
“Until everything is finalised, I would still be cautious. Once it is accepted and announced, then prices are definitely likely to fall as supplies will increase and sanctions on Russian oil will be removed,” said Gnanasekhar Thiagarajan, founder of Commtrendz Research.
Benchmark Brent prices could fall to below US$50 per barrel if the peace deal were to materialise, he said.

Members of Opec+ have been increasing production, with the powerful oil-producing group agreeing in November to a modest output increase of 137,000 barrels per day for December. Further increases are planned for next year, albeit at a slower pace, according to analysts.
The group is due to meet on Sunday to discuss its output plans.
Oil producers have been balancing between producing more oil to boost their revenue and dealing with lower global demand due to economic headwinds.
Asian oil consumers, particularly India and China, have been buying Russian oil at a discount in the wake of the war.
To curb such purchases, Washington has imposed more sanctions on Russian oil producers.
“For India and China, the payoff [from a Russia-Ukraine peace deal] is paradoxical. A peace deal may lower their oil import bills, but also end [their] access to cheap Russian grades,” said Uday Chandra, an assistant professor at Georgetown University in Qatar.
India’s refiners have taken advantage of this by exporting diesel and jet fuel to Europe at full market prices while China has used discounted Russian oil to improve its refining margins, he said.

Moderate impact on prices
While oil prices could fall further in the event of an end to the war, Moscow may end discounts to its Asian customers if Russia were to regain market access to the West, analysts say.
Even if Western sanctions are lifted, Russia may take a while to be in a position to step up its oil supply due to challenges such as securing sufficient container ships and getting financing and insurance support, according to Chandra.
Jamus Lim, an associate professor of economics at ESSEC Business School Asia-Pacific, said that even if a peace deal were imminent, the impact on oil prices would be moderate.
“China and India have enjoyed low-priced oil by driving a hard bargain with Russia after the latter got shut out of Western markets. Since this means that Russian oil was not completely off the market, it also means that its return may not have a major effect on crude prices,” Lim said.
Thiagarajan said heating demand during the winter season and more vehicles on the road in the US towards the year-end could provide some price support.
Oil prices are likely to average around US$60 per barrel next year, although there could be a short-term price rise in the case of any geopolitical tensions, according to Thiagarajan.

data.title: Wang Chuqin and Sun Yingsha unite as mixed doubles wild card at WTT Finals in Hong Kong
data.description:
China table tennis stars Wang Chuqin and Sun Yingsha have been unveiled as the mixed doubles wild-card pairing for the WTT Finals in Hong Kong next month, organisers announced on Thursday.
Hong Kong’s Wong Chun-ting and Doo Hoi-kem will be the city’s only mixed doubles representatives, as one of the seven duos to have qualified through ranking points.
Wang and Sun, who had each already qualified for the singles events, needed the one wild-card place available because of a lack of competitive appearances together in mixed doubles during the season.
Both go into the WTT Finals after competing at the recent National Games, where Wang lost in the men’s singles semi-finals to Fan Zhendong and Sun lost in the women’s singles final to Wang Manyu.
Ranked world No 1 in men’s and women’s singles, Wang and Sun have a strong history of success since they started playing together in 2017.
They made their WTT mixed doubles debut at the 2022 Singapore Grand Slam, are the reigning Olympic mixed doubles gold medallists and won the mixed doubles title at this year’s WTT China Grand Slam.
The Hong Kong finals will mark their 13th appearance in mixed doubles at a WTT series event.
The year’s last major event on the world table tennis calendar, the WTT Finals will take place from December 10 to 14 at the Hong Kong Coliseum.
The tournament will feature 16 men’s and 16 women’s singles players, as well as eight mixed doubles pairs.
The addition of Wang and Sun, two of the sport’s most popular stars, is expected to further raise excitement and anticipation among fans for the tournament.

data.title: Lea Salonga on Disney roles, crying at KPop Demon Hunters and discrimination she’s faced
data.description:
“Crying like a baby” is not the kind of reaction in-flight entertainment usually evokes, but when Lea Salonga was watching KPop Demon Hunters – in which she has a minor singing role – on a recent trip, that was what happened.
“I wept,” recalls the 54-year-old Filipino singer and actress, famed for being the singing voice behind two beloved Disney characters: Jasmine in Aladdin (1992) and the eponymous heroine in the 1998 movie Mulan.
“I watched it on a flight, and I was like, ‘Why is this movie making me cry so much?’”
Another work that made her “cry buckets” was Maybe Happy Ending.
“It’s quite the successful South Korean musical, and then it made its way to Broadway and it won all of the things. It’s such a gem.
“If there’s anything that those productions have proven, it’s that there is a demand for this kind of [Asian] storytelling. There is a demand to learn about another culture.”

Salonga will return to Hong Kong for a special one-night-only concert on January 10, 2026, at Hong Kong Disneyland for the park’s anniversary celebrations, two decades after she performed for the park’s grand opening.
She will sing some of her all-time classics, such as “Reflection” from Mulan, whose lead character is still dear to her heart.
By voicing two landmark Disney heroines, she catalysed a new era of diversity and inclusivity for Asian performers in animation.
“[Mulan] is Asian in the way I am Asian, and it changes the image that says a lot of folks have about people like us. You just never really know what kind of joy it’ll bring to another human being.
“It makes me so happy knowing that there’s going to be this five-year-old listening to that music.”
The KPop Demon Hunters soundtrack has earned five Grammy nominations for 2026, while Maybe Happy Ending won six Tony Awards in 2025. It is this global recognition for Asian-led productions that Salonga says fills her with so much hope.

The project that Salonga is most anticipating is her work on the forthcoming DreamWorks Animation film, Forgotten Island. It will be the studio’s first production to centre on Filipino culture, featuring the story of two best friends in the magical world. She has already begun recording for the film, which is scheduled for release in autumn 2026.
No further information has been officially released, and while Salonga remains tight-lipped about her role, she shares that she fell in love with the character immediately.
The production comes 37 years after Salonga first shattered Broadway’s bamboo ceiling with her performance as Kim in Miss Saigon, during a time that held limited opportunities for Asian performers.
Decades on, she has seen a tangible increase in Asian representation on stage and screen, marking a shift from the early days of her career.
Back then, even as the first Asian actress to receive a Tony Award and the first Filipino to have a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, she faced discrimination in the industry because of her racial background.
“I’ve been refused an audition just because of who I am and where I’m from, and that had never happened to me before, and this was already after I had a Tony in my hand,” she says.
“When you walk into an audition room as a person of colour, you’ve got to be twice as good as everybody else in the room, and you’re not entitled to anything. Be ready to be rejected a million times.”
She has given the same advice to her son Nic, who is also pursuing a career in acting and singing.
“I give him the real score,” she says. “It’s like, just because you’re my kid, do not expect anything to happen.”
Offstage, Salonga has been a vocal advocate for authentic representation. She has publicly criticised Hollywood for “whitewashing” and used her platform during the Covid-19 pandemic to speak out against anti-Asian racism and gendered violence.

She has lent her voice to two key initiatives: a 2020 NBC Asian-American town hall condemning xenophobia, and a 2021 forum, “#AAPI Women Strong”, where she joined US politician Hillary Clinton and others to dissect how xenophobic violence disproportionately affects Asian-American and Pacific Islander women.
She notes a significant surge in visibility for Asian performers on Broadway right now, where productions like Here Lies Love, which Salonga starred in, can feature an entirely Asian cast and where Filipino actors hold principal roles.
“I’d like to think that, thanks to these shows and also Hamilton or Les Misérables, which have cast Asians as leads, it’s not that big of a deal now that Asian actors are in this space, as long as race is not that uncredited character.
“If that can happen for one show, it can happen for another show, so I’m hopeful that we’ll get to see more storytelling from that part of the world. That is what I’m looking for: original, Asian stories.”

data.title: Families facing heartbreaking uncertainty in aftermath of Hong Kong fire tragedy
data.description:
Leaving Prince of Wales Hospital in Sha Tin on Thursday, unaware of the whereabouts of some of his relatives, a 76-year-old man surnamed Lam was left uncertain how to arrange the funeral of his older brother, who had just died in Hong Kong’s worst fire in seven decades.
He arrived at the interdepartmental help desk in the hospital lobby early in the morning to look for his sister-in-law and nephew, who had lived with his brother, 80, in the same flat at Wan Cheong House.
“They haven’t picked up the phone since yesterday. I’m so worried that they are still in the house,” Lam said.
Lam was among scores of families anxiously waiting for news about their loved ones or for updates on those being treated for injuries from the massive fire in Wang Fuk Estate in Tai Po, which had continued to burn for a second day.
As of 2.50pm on Thursday, the death toll had risen to 55, with an unknown number of people still trapped inside the seven residential tower blocks that were ablaze. Only one block had been spared from the inferno.
Earlier in the day, hospital officers told Lam that there were no records showing his sister-in-law and nephew had been admitted.
Lam knew he would have to break the bad news to his other siblings that the pair remained unaccounted for as of 10am.
Asked what he could do next, he said: “We don’t know whether we should do [the funeral] for one or for three.”

Lam recalled receiving the news of his brother’s death on Wednesday evening.
“Life happens,” he said.
Holding a tissue in his hand, he said he wanted to know where his brother’s body had been found – whether he was in the house or in the hallway trying to escape.
The help desk had been set up to assist relatives of the injured and deceased.
A Post reporter observed at least 20 members of the public entering the booth throughout the morning, while officers were seen shaking hands with relatives as a gesture of sympathy.
At the emergency department, ambulances carrying rescued residents arrived at about 10.30am, one after another.
Patients brought in from the scene were easily identified by a card attached to their bodies.
What distinguished them from other patients was the colour of their skin, their faces and limbs appearing slightly darker – almost as if coated in grey ash.
Three patients were breathing through oxygen masks and lying on stretchers with their eyes closed.
There was a distinct odour in the air as the ambulance doors opened.
The burnt smell lingered briefly even after the patients were wheeled into the emergency room.
